questions.question: What does a strong cached consistency answer begin with?
options: A user-visible freshness or read-after-write promise for the critical path.
A cache vendor selection table only.
The maximum possible TTL for every object.
A decision to avoid replication discussion.
questions.explanation: Consistency in cached systems should be explained in terms of what users observe, not just infrastructure terms.

questions.question: Why is 'eventual consistency' alone often too vague in an interview?
options: It does not say who may see stale data, for how long, or after which failures.
It is always the wrong choice for caches.
It means the cache has no TTL.
It only applies to SQL databases.
questions.explanation: The interviewer wants the actual consistency envelope, not a generic label.

questions.question: What technique can help provide monotonic reads for a user after a write?
options: Session stickiness or a minimum-version read requirement.
Only increasing the cache size.
Removing invalidation events.
Using FIFO eviction.
questions.explanation: Monotonic-read behavior often needs routing or version-awareness beyond a plain TTL cache.

questions.question: Which metric best detects critical stale-read problems?
options: Read-after-write mismatch rate or cache-versus-source version skew.
Only total memory allocated to the cache.
Average document length in the repo.
The number of dashboards in Grafana.
questions.explanation: Mismatch and version skew directly reveal when cached state diverges in user-visible ways.

questions.question: What is a common trade-off of adding version-aware cache bypass on critical reads?
options: Better correctness where it matters, at the cost of extra metadata and selective miss traffic.
Lower memory use and stronger freshness everywhere automatically.
No need for observability anymore.
Guaranteed global serializability.
questions.explanation: Selective strictness is powerful, but it is not free.

questions.question: When is bounded stale data often acceptable?
options: For noncritical content where lower latency and offload matter more than immediate post-write visibility.
For every safety-critical policy path.
Only when the cache is in the browser.
Never in any production system.
questions.explanation: Many cached systems deliberately accept bounded staleness for convenience data.

questions.question: Which interview answer is strongest?
options: I will say the cache is eventually consistent and move on.
I will name which entities allow bounded stale reads, which require read-after-write guarantees, and what mechanism enforces that difference.
I will disable all caches after any write globally.
I will treat all reads as equally critical.
questions.explanation: Strong answers classify guarantees by user impact and explain the enforcing mechanism.

questions.question: What hidden issue appears when users bounce between regions quickly?
options: They may see time go backward unless session affinity or version-aware reads preserve monotonic behavior.
All cache layers automatically synchronize instantly.
Eviction policy stops mattering.
The origin no longer needs replication.
questions.explanation: Cross-region movement can expose freshness differences that a single-region design never surfaces.
